turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in Case 15짯1808, the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico v. Valle. Mr. Landau.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case turns on the straightforward point that the people of Puerto Rico are the source of authority for the laws of Puerto Rico. That means that a prior Federal conviction has no Double Jeopardy implications for the enforcement of the Commonwealth's criminal laws because Commonwealth law and Federal law emanate from different sources of authority: The people of Puerto Rico on the one hand and Congress on the other.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Landau, could Congress amend 48 U.S.C. 1704, which covers Guam, the Virgin Islands, American Samoa? Could it amend that statute and put Puerto Rico in there as well?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: It could certainly amend that statute, Your Honor, with respect to Federal prosecutions in Puerto Rico pursuant to its authority over Federal prosecutors. Your question, Your Honor, raises a very intricate question with 짯짯 with 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: In 짯짯 in the statute is 짯짯 it works both ways.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: In the statute, Your Honor, it does because there's no question Puerto Rico is in a unique status different than Guam, the Virgin Islands, and American Samoa precisely because those are all territories governed, as traditionally, by organic acts of Congress.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But is there 짯짯 is there 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: What 짯짯 what about the Northern Marianas?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Your Honor, they are in an interesting position that is generally more analogous to Puerto Rico in the sense that they are a Commonwealth of the Northern Marianas with a compact of their own that was very much modeled on Puerto Rico, although it's somewhat different than the Puerto Rico model. But there's a profound distinction that goes to the heart of this case between the home rule territories, were Congress, to be sure, has delegated a measure of self짯government to those particular territories, but each of them, Guam, the Virgin Islands, and American Samoa are still governed by organic acts of Congress, as was Puerto Rico prior to 1952.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Landau, could you finish your answer to my question? You said yes, as far as what the federal Congress can deal with, what the federal prosecutors do, but the statute works both ways. Are you saying it couldn't 짯짯 Congress has no power to do that with respect to Puerto Rico?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: I would say, Your Honor, that that raises a very interesting and tricky issue with respect to the compact that the 1950 Public Law 600 offered the people of Puerto Rico that was accepted. As this Court said in the Flores de Otero case in the 1970s 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: You're saying it's a hard question, but you haven't given me 짯짯

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Well, I 짯짯 I 짯짯 I think the answer is probably not, insofar as this Court said 짯짯 and I'm not saying this myself. I'm quoting this Court. Congress relinquished control over the organization of the internal affairs of the Island. Now, one point to make very, very clear: Our position today on the double jeopardy issue does not turn on that relinquishing issue. But to answer your hypothetical specifically, if Congress started to tell the Puerto Rico prosecutors what cases they may prosecute, that might raise some serious questions under 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Just take the statute as it is. Just add Puerto Rico 짯짯

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Right 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: 짯짯 not telling prosecutors what to do in a particular case.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Well, and 짯짯 and, again, Your Honor, I think the critical point here, and I think what is most telling going back to some of the colloquy that just happened in the other case, Congress since 1952 has never attempted to do anything like that, to tell the people 짯짯 to exercise control over what the government officials in Puerto Rico 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That doesn't mean it couldn't. That doesn't mean it couldn't change the law. Is it essential to your case that we recognize Puerto Rico as a sovereign?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: It is not essential that you recognize Puerto Rico as a sovereign with a capital "S," because if you get 12 political 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's our usual double jeopardy law, isn't it?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: It 짯짯 it is 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 짯짯 two sovereigns, it's a different matter.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: It is the shorthand that this Court has typically used, the dual sovereignty doctrine. But the court made clear this is the lesson of the Wheeler case where the court was 짯짯 the Ninth Circuit said, gee, these Indian tribes don't look like sovereigns to us, because they are subject to the plenary control of Congress. That's not what we typically think of as sovereigns. And this Court says you're missing the point, Ninth Circuit. What we mean in the context of double jeopardy, the language we are construing is the same offense. Laws 짯짯 offences created by two different entities are not the same offense if they flow from different sources of court.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, I don't understand how that helps you, Mr. Landau. You're saying that the test is what the source of authority is, and you're saying that the source of authority here is the Puerto Rican people.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But that seems to be just 짯짯 you're not taking another step back. What's the source of authority of Puerto Rican people? The source of authority of Puerto Rican people was a Congressional act. If you go back, the ultimate source of authority is Congress.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: That, Your Honor, is the crux of the case. And with all respect, I disagree with the suggestion that the ultimate source here is Congress. Congress could be 짯짯 what Your Honor just described is very much like a home rule jurisdiction, where, let's say, you have the Virgin Islands legislature that passes a Virgin Islands law. But that government of the Virgin Islands is itself a creature of Congress, as was the government of the Philippines in the early 1900s, and Puerto Rico in '30s at the time of the Shell case. What made all the difference in the world, Your Honor, was in 1950 to '52 Congress said, we recognize and fully endorse the government 짯짯 the concept of government by consent. So we are not delegating authority to 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, but Congress today, if it felt like it 짯짯 and of course it won't. But if it felt like it, could Congress go back on that decision?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Well, this then goes to the relinquishment issue, Your Honor, in terms of what is the nature of the compact. This has been a very emotional and hot짯button issue in Puerto Rico. As Chief Judge McGruder noted in his article in 1953 already, there were divergent views at the time from the very outset on whether 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, suppose we assume that Congress could rescind the existing compact.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Do you then lose your case?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Absolutely not, Your Honor. And that is 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Because this is, again, the lesson of Wheeler. It was uncontested in Wheeler that plenary congressional control over the Indians 짯짯 over the Indian tribes meant that Congress could abrogate Indian sovereign immunity with the stroke of a pen, and even abrogate the tribes and derecognize the tribes. But that didn't mean that the existing tribal laws at the time were not considered the laws of the separate tribes 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Are there 짯짯 you know, "sovereignty" is a slippery word. That's why the framers didn't use it in the constitution. Are you suggesting there's kind of a second class sovereignty and a first class sovereignty?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I 짯짯 I couldn't agree more with your insight, that "sovereignty" is a slippery word. If you got 12 political philosophers, they would all give you a different answer as to what "sovereignty" means. And I think that is precisely why Wheeler said we can't have judges and courts trying to decide double jeopardy questions by asking abstract questions of sovereignty, because, you know, even the State sovereignty is different than the sovereignty of the independent nations. They don't have ambassadors, et cetera. So what this Court has made clear is that the test in the double jeopardy context is all about the source of authority for the laws. And going back to Justice Kagan's question, which I think cuts to the heart of the case, in this particular case Congress said, we 짯짯 and this is pursuant to demands for 짯짯 by the people of Puerto Rico for government by consent. The people of Puerto Rico said, we are tired of being a colony. We want to create our own government. And so the Constitution of Puerto Rico could not be more explicit on this score, that it says, "The political power of the Commonwealth emanates from the people." That document was submitted to the Puerto Rico voters. So you're a Puerto Rico voter and you say this is what I'm voting for. It was submitted to the President of the United States and Congress, who approved that particular 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Landau, we have a situation as a result of the decision of the Supreme Court of Puerto Rico. I think it was two of the justices who said we're not going to get into this business about the Constitution. We think, interpreting our own double jeopardy provision, somebody should not be tried a second time. So that's going to be the law of Puerto Rico. That's 짯짯 if the Puerto Rico Supreme Court said that, then we would have the situation that has led us to grant cert in this case. That is, if you have a prosecution first in the Federal government, then Puerto Rico will not have a second prosecution. You have it first in Puerto Rico. The Federal government is free to institute a second prosecution.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: You're absolutely 짯짯 you're absolutely right, Your Honor. And that was the position of two of the nine justices on the Supreme Court of Puerto Rico. That position did not command a majority, which is why there's no adequate and independent State ground here. The majority was very explicit 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And they could. They could. If 짯짯 if the 짯짯 if we told the majority you're wrong, nothing would prevent them from agreeing with those two justices, right?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: No, that's absolutely right. And 짯짯 and about 21 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Then there's no way you could break that situation of having 짯짯 it depends on which 짯짯 which jurisdiction goes first.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. And that 짯짯 that is true in about 21 states, that as a matter of State law, either statutory or constitutional, do not allow a subsequent prosecution. But again, that doesn't change the point. What 짯짯 what made a cert grant appropriate and intolerable was that the Federal 짯짯 was the difference between the Federal and State courts in Puerto Rico on the federal constitutional question. It was not if one were Federal and one were State.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Landau, there's something you said that resonates with me, 'cause I've been trying to find a definition of "sovereignty." And one has been created in the briefing by saying that states are sovereign; yet if you look at international usage, they wouldn't consider states necessarily sovereign because they can't order their foreign affairs. They can't print money. They can't do lots of things that others would consider them sovereign for. So I guess the Constitution does something else with that word. What is it 짯짯 what are the elements of sovereignty with respect to the Double Jeopardy Clause that you think are commanded by our case law? There 짯짯 there's more than just the Puerto Rican people say.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And it can't be that it's the compact alone. So what is it? What are the principles 짯짯

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 짯짯 that you meet that would create sovereignty in the Double Jeopardy sense?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: To 짯짯 to take your 짯짯 your question in turn, Your Honor, this Court has made it clear in a series of cases starting in Wheeler in 1978 that really tries to synthesize what the Court has been saying with respect to the dual sovereignty doctrine over a century. And what the Court said is, in the Double Jeopardy context, we are trying to decide whether two offenses from different entities are the same offense. That has traditionally been called "dual sovereignty." But what we mean by "dual sovereignty" in this context doesn't require this broader 짯짯 let's say 짯짯 I'll call it "sovereignty with a capital S" inquiry. It is a much more targeted and narrow inquiry into 짯짯 is the 짯짯 the source of authority for each offense.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Now, I'm looking at Lanza, and it says 짯짯 I think Lanza says, and it's a Double Jeopardy 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 짯짯 Clause, that for Double Jeopardy Clause purposes, sovereignty means, one, that the separate entity possesses the authority to determine what shall be an 짯짯 an offense against their peace and dignity; two, can enact laws without the interference of the other; and, three, draws authorities to punish the offender 짯짯 and this is the one that you've been arguing 짯짯 from a distinct source of power from the other sovereign.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And 짯짯 and you keep saying it's the Constitution. It's the "We the People." It sounds a 짯짯 a bit histrionic to me. It 짯짯 there's something else. What is meant 짯짯

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: It is, Your Honor. And I 짯짯 and I 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 짯짯 by this distinct source of power?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: If 짯짯 if could just say 짯짯 make two quick points in response to that. First, Lanza is not the last word on this because Wheeler, in the '70s, clarified what Lanza meant because, for instance, the Ninth Circuit in Wheeler said, well, the Indian Tribes don't fit the Lanza definition because Congress has plenary authority to overrule at any time. So that goes to the first part of your question. To the second part of your question, it is Congress acting in tandem with the people of Puerto Rico that's critical. In other words, it is certainly not the people of Puerto Rico that could unilaterally and without reference to Congress just say, guess what? We are the source of authority of our own laws. And 짯짯 and that would be the end of the story.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Landau, I thought that, in the 짯짯 in the case of the Indian Tribes, what 짯짯 what the Court said is they were once sovereign before we got here and we took some of that sovereignty away. That's not the case with Puerto Rico.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Yes. We are certainly not saying that we are absolutely on all fours with the Indian Tribes. There are 짯짯 what Your Honor said is true. Although one thing to keep in mind, of course, is that not all tribes proceeded the 짯짯 the 짯짯 the creation of the United States. In fact, if Congress can recognize Indian Tribes to this day, the standard is under 25 C.F.R. 83.7. And an entity that existed as recently at 1900 can still be considered an Indian tribe. So 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But the rationale was that the tribes had this sovereignty, and that's what was being respected.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: That 짯짯 that 짯짯 that is correct, Your Honor. The 짯짯 the 짯짯 the tribes had an inherent sovereignty. But I think the point for Double Jeopardy purposes doesn't require the inherency. It just requires a 짯짯 a recognition by Congress of an exercise of sovereignty. And that goes, I think 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, what is it? That is, look. If we simply write an opinion and it says, Puerto Rico is sovereign, that has enormous implications.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: It does.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: The insular cases are totally changed in their applications.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: The political implications I'll just stay away from. On the other hand, if we write an opinion that says it's just a territory, that has tremendous implications.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: How did we tell the UN it wasn't a colony? Why are we not reporting on this colony every year?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So either way, between those two, the implications in law and in politics and everything else are overwhelming. Therefore, you argue a third and middle position. Your position pointing to four cases in this Court, so you have very good authority. It's for Double Jeopardy purposes. There is a different question. The question is what are the sources of the law? And then I find four cases that say just what you said. Okay? Now, you say the sources of the law, the sources of criminal law here are different. Okay. What I think Justice Sotomayor wanted you to say is explain that.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Take a little time, if you like. In what way are they different?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Absolutely, Your Honor. They are different, Your Honor, because Congress invited 짯짯 in Public Law 600, Congress said, we recognize the principle of government by consent. That is something that we, as Congress rightly 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Going back to the political issues. And 짯짯 and 짯짯 and that's what I think Justice Breyer is trying to stay away from. This is a very simple question.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right? Can the Federal government override a Puerto Rican law?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Your Honor 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Can they 짯짯

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: This goes to 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Can they veto a Puerto Rican law?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: No, absolutely not.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: They cannot.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Who makes these laws?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: The people of Puerto Rico 짯짯 well, the legislative assembly, the 짯짯 the 짯짯 the legislative powers of which were vested by the people of Puerto Rico in the legislative assembly. In other words, the Puerto Rico Constitution could not be more specific in saying "We, the people of Puerto Rico, in exercise of our" 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The laws were made by Puerto Rico's comparable Congress.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. And 짯짯 and this is the 짯짯 I think the point I'd really like to underscore, because I think it's critical here, is this arrangement was not something that Puerto Rico did as a rogue usurpation of authority. This was pursuant to the invitation of Congress and with the blessing of Congress. That was submitted to the Congress. The Congress saw that language.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Even in saying that, Mr. Landau, you're putting Congress in the driver's seat here: It was done at the invitation of Congress. Congress approved it. Presumably Congress can unapprove it if Congress ever wished to. So if Congress is in the driver's seat, why isn't Congress the source of authority for the purposes of our Double Jeopardy jurisprudence 짯짯

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Because 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 짯짯 which seems to make that the issue? I mean, you can imagine a different Double Jeopardy jurisprudence where the issue was who just exercises authority in the real world? But that seems not to be what we ask.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: That's 짯짯 that's correct. That's correct. And I think that 짯짯 that the key point that I'd like to make, Your Honor, is that you have to look at 짯짯 Congress has plenary authority over the territories under the Territorial Clause. Our position is that Congress is not the prisoner of its plenary authority; it is the master of plenary authority. And therefore, when Congress can decide that, for the long짯term future of Puerto Rico, it does not think it is appropriate or good for 짯짯 for Puerto Rico or the United States to have direct or delegated Federal power in Puerto Rico. It says we accede to the 짯짯 the request of the Puerto Rican people to create their own government and to be the source of authority of their own law. So that's what Congress invites. The people of Puerto Rico accept the invitation. They enact a Constitution that is entirely explicit saying the political power of the Commonwealth creates the structure, creates a legislative authority, vests the courts of Puerto Rico with judicial authority 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Let 짯짯 let's call this 짯짯 this theory of yours interim sovereignty. Are there any examples in international law of interim sovereignty? Are there any examples in international law or in the United States' experience of a dichotomy such as you suggest?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Your Honor, again, I think 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I 짯짯 I 짯짯 I just don't know the answer to that.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: I think that 짯짯 that 짯짯 that, in a sense, the danger of that is that's already going back to asking about concepts of sovereignty. And I think the more one asks those more abstract questions, it gets away from what is a much simpler question, which is 짯짯 I think this goes back to Justice Sotomayor's point, which is what is 짯짯 we have before us here 짯짯 we 짯짯 this is a specific case or controversy. Nobody's asking the Court to make a broad political statement. All we want to know is may Puerto Rico, may the Commonwealth prosecutors prosecute these particular Puerto Rico Commonwealth gun charges or 짯짯 and ammunition charges? Okay? The 짯짯 the source of authority for these particular laws is the legislature of Puerto Rico, and that legislative authority does not come from delegated power from Congress.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Why does that 짯짯

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: That's what distinguishes this from municipalities 짯짯 I'm sorry.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Why does 짯짯 why does the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico have that authority? Where did it come from?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: The 짯짯 Congress can recognize and invite an exercise of sovereignty, just like this Court recognized in Lara.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: But it had complete authority under the Territorial Clause whether to do that or not, right?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: That's correct. And that is exactly 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So that would 짯짯 I 짯짯 it would seem to me, then, that the authority to do it came from Congress when they passed the 짯짯

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: But 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: 짯짯 the law authorizing Puerto Rico to adopt a Constitution of its own.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: But 짯짯 but this 짯짯 you just said authorizing. I think the key point is it invited Puerto Rico. And 짯짯 and what it did is the Constitution that Puerto Rico adopted and that Congress then accepted specifically says political power emanates from the people. So Your Honor, I think 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Why is 짯짯 I mean, you seem to fix on invited as somehow different than authorized.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Well, even if you said 짯짯 I guess the point is authorized sounds like a delegation of authority. I think the point is Congress said, look, you go adopt your own constitution. That in and of itself doesn't necessarily answer the question of where the authority from that constitution comes from. But again, when it says fully recognizing the principal of government by consent, this is what the people of Puerto Rico wanted. They wanted to 짯짯 to create their own government. They didn't want to have another Organic Act.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Aren't there territorial legislatures?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: There are indeed, Your Honor. In 짯짯 in 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What's the difference?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: The difference is 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Why 짯짯 why can't you say the laws they enact have as their source the 짯짯 the people of the territory?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: You could. And in fact, that was the tradition in the 19th century. We cited a number of cases 짯짯 of course there weren't a lot of these kind of cases in the 19th century because the Federal government had very, very limited criminal power. So these come up in things like counterfeiting money and selling liquor to the Indians. But 짯짯 but so 짯짯 so the 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: May I just ask you a question about that? I don't quite understand because this 짯짯 this whole issue is a result of a five짯to짯four decision of this Court in the 1950s, right? Bartkus.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Bartkus.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: But yeah, I mean, certainly 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So 짯짯 so talking about cases before that, there was just 짯짯 there was no issue before that.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, certainly the 짯짯 the principle of dual sovereignty had been recognized by this Court as early as the 1840s; in other words, that 짯짯 that two offenses for Double Jeopardy purposes were not the same if 짯짯 if they were created by different entities with something that this Court had recognized, albeit in 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Recognized how, because this was 짯짯 it was a very close case, as you know.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Absolutely. Absolutely. And again 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And so I don't think it was settled before then.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Well, but it 짯짯 it was something that 짯짯 it has been an issue that had divided people in the Court. In the more recent cases since the 1950s, it has not been a particularly divisive issue. And just to be clear in this case, there is no call by the other side to overrule 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: I'm questioning you looking back before the Bartkus case, for precedent.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Right. But Bartkus didn't come out of the blue, Your Honor. Bartkus has antecedents. Lanza was the first case in 1922 when prohibition is what really suddenly had federal criminal laws that were quite widespread throughout the country. And Lanza is, I think, the first case you can characterize as a square holding of this case on the dual sovereignty doctrine. It continued to be a somewhat controversial doctrine up until the 1950s, and then it's been, I think, settled since then. And it's not been challenged in this case.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Landau, I'm not sure you answered my question about why territorial legislatures are different.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: They are different because those 짯짯 the other ones are Home Rule legislatures. In other words, there's a Virgin Islands Organic Act, there's a Guam Organic Act. So Congress 짯짯 and a D.C. Home Rule Act. Congress created that government structure and endowed it with authority. Pursuant to what Congress did, the people have a certain degree of autonomy. They elect their legislators. D.C. has D.C. laws. The Virgin Islands has Virgin Islands law. You know, to some extent or other those can be vetoed, maybe, by Congress.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: If you say that the issue is the source of the law in question, it seems to me that the territorial legislature is as much the source of a law as is the legislature of Puerto Rico.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: The question, Your Honor, is the ultimate source of authority for the law. So when you have a Home Rule jurisdiction 짯짯 again, this is 짯짯 I'm not saying that this is what this Court has held, that when you have a Home Rule jurisdiction 짯짯 this is the Waller case, for instance, where it's a municipality, was clearly 짯짯 the city council was the source of the law. But the ultimate source of the law was delegated power from the State. The critical point here is that the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico, the legislative assembly of Puerto Rico 짯짯 this goes back to Justice Sotomayor's point and Justice Kagan's point 짯짯 is not exercising power delegated by Congress 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I see your 짯짯 I see your time is getting up. It seemed to me that in a way, if you answered Justice Scalia's question, well, yes, it is 짯짯 it is true that local legislature is the source of the law, and there is Double Jeopardy there too. Yes.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Well, you 짯짯 again, that would be another way of looking at the 짯짯 at the issue. And that, frankly, was the historical way of looking at it. If you go back to the 1850s cases, they said territories, which at that time were creatures of Congress, that was considered to be a separate sovereign for Double Jeopardy. That's the historical antecedent. But in the 20th century, particularly in Wheeler and the more recent cases, the Court has said the question is the ultimate source of authority. The ultimate source of authority for the laws of Puerto Rico, unlike the ultimate source of authority for the laws of the Virgin Islands, is the people of Puerto Rico, not Congress. I'd like to reserve the balance of my time.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Unikowsky.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Under our Constitution, States are sovereign and territories are not. Although Puerto Rico has indisputedly achieved a historic degree of autonomy, it remains a territory under Article IV. As such, it cannot be considered sovereign for Double Jeopardy purposes. An unbroken line of this Court's Double Jeopardy cases has stated, both before and after the enactment of the 1952 constitution of Puerto Rico, that territories are not sovereign for Double Jeopardy purposes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: It does seem, Mr. Unikowsky, as if Congress has given Puerto Rico as much authority as it possibly could have short of making it a State itself. Do you disagree with that?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, two responses. One, yes, I disagree with that. And two, even if I agreed with that, we do think that there is a sharp dividing line between States and territories for constitutional purposes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What kind of territory is it?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: It is a 짯짯 we think there's only one type of territory 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: There is. The 짯짯 the insular cases have at least two.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, yes. The insular cases that hold that it is considered an unincorporated territory. That is true.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay. So it's that kind.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Now, what is Estado Libre Asociado?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Your Honor, I believe that's a case that just construed a Federal statute to hold 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No, no. I just wonder as it appears in the Constitution of Puerto Rico.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: The alternative of the word Commonwealth.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I just wonder what it is.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: We believe that the Constitution does not include that as a constitutional category, just as it doesn't 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Where does it say in the Constitution that Congress cannot? After all, Congress can admit a State. It says that specifically.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: It certainly does 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Talks about territories. So you're saying Congress and the President too do not have the authority to associate with some other entity under the form of Estado Libre Asociado?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Where does it say that in any of our cases?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, I think the Yankton case very clearly says that if a portion of land is not in a State, then it has to be under a territory.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And what are the Indians?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Your Honor, the Indian tribes are a sui generis category explicitly recognized in the Constitution 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Because 짯짯 because we do not recognize explicitly in the Constitution "Commonwealth," therefore there is no Commonwealth? Is that what you want us to say?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I want you to say that this Court has already held in the Harris case that Puerto Rico is a territory. And not only that, that 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: With some purposes. But the 짯짯 the issue here, as I see it 짯짯 which maybe will take a second 짯짯 is there are four cases that say we don't have to reach these grand questions. All we have to do is decide what the source of power is. Now their argument is that even if you go back to the Foraker Act, which indeed did have the people of Puerto Rico making laws, and if you then add the Resolution 600 which delegated the authority to make the Constitution, the Constitution itself which speaks of "We the People" of Puerto Rico making a law, the fact that later Congress and the President said Puerto Rico has a Republican form of government, the fact that subsequent to that we went to the United Nations and had them withdraw the requirement to report on a colony because Puerto Rico is not a colony 짯짯 짯 and you know the words as well as I, and they sure sound like "sovereign." And in Valle, which no one mentions, Trias Monge wrote that in fact all these laws 짯짯 and he was talking about civil, but I suppose it applies to Commonwealth too 짯짯 are to be interpreted in light of the civil code tradition of Europe, which was the tradition that applied prior to 1900, and not the common law. I don't see, when you put all those things together, if you're looking at the facts of what the law of Puerto Rico is in the area, it sounds to me like it's civil code coming out of a constitution which I grant you was given by authority of Congress. So there we are. Now five things they've listed there that make it different, not only from anything you can think of but from anything I can think of. So why don't you reply to those five things, if you want.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Your Honor, those five things established 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Take your time.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I think those features of Puerto Rico and others established that Puerto Rico has undoubtedly achieved a significant degree of autonomy, but those characteristics are not the characteristics that matter for sovereignty purposes or for Double Jeopardy purposes. The question is the ultimate source of power, and here the ultimate source of power is Congress, which delegated the authority to enact the Puerto Rican Constitution. And I'd also point out that if one looks at the characteristics of sovereignty as defined in the Constitution and this Court's cases, Puerto Rico doesn't have them. For instance, in the Alden cases, this Court explained one of the reasons we call States sovereign is that one cannot frame their generally 짯짯 general police power as the delegation of Federal power because the Federal government doesn't have a general police power in the States, so there's nothing to delegate. That's just not true in Puerto Rico where 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that was not true of many of the territories that were admitted as States. They came in to Statehood and were conferred by a 짯짯 the sovereignty was conferred by a 짯짯 agreement with the Federal government. And States were admitted by agreement, and sovereignty were confirmed on them by agreement. And so are you telling me that if 짯짯 let's say one of the islands in the Caribbean. I'll make one up. Okay? Atlantis has been 짯짯 never kicked out its foreign colonial status 200 years ago, but it really doesn't have many resources. And it comes to the United States and it says, I want a treaty. You'll take care of all of our external affairs. We'll follow whatever you say with respect to external affairs. We're even going to use the American dollar 'cause it's convenient, but you can't touch our internal affairs. And Congress approves that treaty. Would that, for Double Jeopardy purposes, not be a sovereign?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I think it wouldn't be a sovereign if that was not part of the United States, not subject 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I couldn't hear. Would or wouldn't?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: It wouldn't. So for instance, we can see that Micronesia and Palau, which are not independent countries, I believe in the United Nations, are 짯짯 are genuinely sovereign, even though there are defense agreements with the United States because the United States has foreign allies with whom they have many treaties and relationships, and those foreign allies may be sovereign, but those aren't territories. They're not 짯짯 the American flag doesn't fly over them, they're not subject to plenary power of Congress and Federal law. That's the distinction in Puerto Rico, which is, indisputably, a territory 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But if Congress has that treaty power, why does it have similar power under its Rule and Regulation Clause of the Constitution? There's no limiting principle of what rules and regulations Congress can make.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, let me refer, Your Honor, both to 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Under that 짯짯

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I apologize. JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR 짯짯 under that constitutional clause, there's no suggestion of how or even what kinds of treaties 짯짯 perhaps not in violation of the Constitution, but what kind of treaties Congress can make. And it makes an awful large number of them.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: It does, Your Honor. But let me refer to both the constitutional text and structure as the basis of our argument. Beginning with the text, Article IV characterizes territories as, quote, "belonging to the United States." And we believe that is antithetical to the concept of sovereignty. Congress has the ultimate power to enact a wide variety of the governmental forms in the territories, but the sine qua non of a territory is that, in fact, Congress does possess that power. And I'd also refer to the constitutional structure. The framers of our Constitution split the atom of sovereignty between the Federal government and the States. And I think that presupposes that the question of what types of sovereigns would exist in the United States is a question of constitutional 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: The Indians. What about the Indians?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Unikowsky, it sure seems as though in the early 1950s, Congress with respect to Puerto Rico, said we want to give it some sovereign authority. We want to give it an enormous amount of Home Rule authority, basically everything, and we also have some idea in our heads that Puerto Rico ought to be a sovereign with all the things sovereigns have like a Constitution and a "We the People" clause. And 짯짯 and why isn't that something 짯짯 it's 짯짯 it's an unusual idea, to be sure, a 짯짯 a sovereign territory. But Congress seems to have wanted to do exactly that.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, I'd first push back on your premise. I don't think that's what Congress wanted to do. I think that the history, the legislative history, and the 짯짯 the events, the historical record, show that Congress wanted to delegate autonomy, which is different from creating a sovereign. So first I'd 짯짯 I'd turn to the issue of Section 20 in which Congress just unceremoniously stripped a portion of the Puerto Rican Constitution and permanently barred Puerto Ricans from enacting 짯짯 reenacting it. That is not consistent with what I think we ordinarily consider a sovereign. And I'd also point to the legislative history and record. I mean, there's testimony from the Secretary of the Interior, from The Resident Commissioner of Puerto Rico, from the Governor of Puerto Rico, in the House report, in the Senate report. All of that seemed to contemplate that the political status of Puerto Rico wouldn't change and this was just a delegation. I mean, there was actually a hearing we quote on page 30 of our brief where the chairman of the relevant Senate committee basically tells everybody that, in fact, Congress's powers over Puerto Rico wouldn't be altered at all; that ultimately this was a delegation of power that one 짯짯 that was revoked and wouldn't change Congress's ultimate power over Puerto Rico. So I actually think it's Petitioner's position that really is fundamentally inconsistent with the historical record as it existed at the time. We don't dispute that there's 짯짯 this is historic legislation. I mean, it's true. Petitioner's brief characterizes the 1950 to '52 legislation as, quote, "path marking." And we actually agree with that, it was path marking. It was historic legislation that delegated a significant amount of power to the people of Puerto Rico, and it was very historically important, and remains important today. But there is a difference, a meaningful constitutional difference between the delegation of power and the conferral of sovereignty. The former occurred in Puerto Rico, as it has occurred in other territories. The latter can only apply to States within our union.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And is 짯짯 is our argument so abstract that it doesn't acknowledge real practicalities of multiple prosecutions? Of 짯짯 of course Mr. Landau couldn't come up and say, please forget Heath and Wheeler and Walter and Grafton. Let's do something other than sovereignty. Has there been any suggestion by commentators and so forth that this whole inquiry of sovereignty and source of power is a little bit misplaced? Of course, you have the problem with the cities within its 짯짯 within separate 짯짯 within one State, which is, it seems, ought to be subject to a single rule. But have there been any commentaries that lead us to another approach altogether? I don't think we're going to overrule four cases, but 짯짯

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I will acknowledge that there has been some larger articles suggesting that this whole line of cases is wrong and the Court should look to something else, to autonomy. But certainly this Court's cases, going back a century, haven't followed that approach at all. And I actually think the municipality example is a very good one for us because cities regularly enact Home Rule charters through a very similar type of Democratic process that Petitioner describes for Puerto Rico. The fundamental principle of Petitioner's position here is that the enactment of a 짯짯 of a charter of local self짯government setting forth the parameters for local self짯government in and of itself leads to sovereignty. That's how they distinguish the Shell case. But if that's true, the 짯짯 there's no limiting principal because municipalities do that all the time. I didn't fully understand 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Unikowsky, you 짯짯 you said before that Congress didn't mean to confer sovereignty here. Do you think Congress could do that?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No, Your Honor, it could not. We think that the Constitution prohibits that, and Congress's exercise of its powers was fully consistent with that constitutional requirement.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. You 짯짯 you're saying to me that Congress can't make Puerto Rico independent?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Of course it could, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So if it can do that, why can't it have other arrangements with Puerto Rico the way it has with everybody else? Why are we saying that with respect to territories they don't have that power?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Your Honor, I think if Congress 짯짯 if Puerto Rico is to remain a territory, which it is under Article IV, as this Court has held, then Congress must retain the ultimate power. I think that follows from the constitutional structure in which the framers contemplated splitting the assets up 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: We need to. I mean, we told the UN, this doesn't sound like we said it was a municipality.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Your Honor 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: UN said that Puerto Rico has been invested with the attributes of political sovereignty, which clearly identify the status of self짯government attained by the Puerto Rican people.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Who said this? This is the UN?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: This is what we told the UN. We told the UN that 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And on the basis of that 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Who 짯짯 who is "we"?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It's 짯짯 what it says here 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Who's we?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It consists of the President of the United States.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The President said that?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Or his delegate. His delegate.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We need to involve counsel in the dialogue.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I 짯짯 I have. You've read the same thing. There are about ten words such as "republic," "not a colony," "attributes of political sovereignty," repeated in five or six key documents. I do 짯짯 not everyone does 짯짯 happen to think that what we tell the UN to get it removed from the colony status bears some consideration. I grant you not everyone agrees with that, but that's my view of it. Laughter.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right. And I guess I 짯짯 all right. Forget it.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Your Honor, we don't see any inconsistency between what the State Department said and our position today 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: You said it was like a municipality. I just think that having the attributes of political sovereignty, and being a republic, and saying that the Constitution of Puerto Rico is the basic document, whatever the language was there, don't sound like a municipality.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Your Honor, there are differences between a municipality and Puerto Rico, such as Puerto Rico has a much more delegated power than the typical municipality. That is undoubtedly true. But the 짯짯 there's no 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You think that doesn't have meaning? In most municipalities, their offenses are treated as criminal offenses and not criminal infractions. They can only pass laws with respect to a very limited amount of issues. The States have control over many, many other things. They are substantially different. Why would we have bothered talking about the structure of Florida's system with municipalities in Waller? We went through how the State controlled all of the 짯짯 most of the internal affairs of municipalities, and we said they are not separate sovereigns because of this control. All we could have said is what you want us to say now, which is municipalities' ultimate source is the State government. But we did something very different in that case.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Your Honor, may I answer?

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Briefly.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I think that, already as of Shell, Puerto Rico enjoyed that broad police power. That's why Petitioner's argument turns entirely on the existence of the Constitution of Puerto Rico. And our point is that the act of Constitution making, we don't think, can confer sovereignty.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Ms. Saharsky. FOR UNITED STATES, AS AMICUS CURIAE, SUPPORTING THE RESPONDENTS

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I think to respond to some of the questions that have come up, I'd like to go back and address what the Court has said this test is, for dual sovereignty in the Double Jeopardy context, and why it's been using that test. And the test that the Court has consistently used for the past 100 years is by looking to the ultimate source of the authority, the laws for prosecuting and the authority to prosecute. And the reason that the Court has said that it's looked to that ultimate source is because the dual sovereignty doctrine rests on the basic structure of our Federal system. That's what the Court said in Wheeler. And the Court has been very careful to guard who is a sovereign under the Constitution and who is not, because, after all, we're talking about a weighty power here, which is the ability of two sovereigns to prosecute a person for an offense with the same elements. So if you trace the history of what this Court has done, it started with Lanza and Grafton. Lanza was a case about the Federal government as opposed to the States. And the Court said should they have this power to dual prosecute? Well, we look to the Constitution, and the unique structure that was put in place: The splitting the atom of sovereignty. And we are in a circumstance where both the States and the Federal government have this sovereign power. They should both be able to prosecute. But then the case 짯짯 the Court turned to cases about the territories, which was the Shell case about Puerto Rico, but then also the Grafton case about the Philippines, and it said, no, the territories derive their ultimate power from Congress. That's true by virtue of the Territory Clause. Now, one thing that the Court has considered in the course of those cases as they've evolved, I think, is the suggestion that Justice Kennedy made, which is maybe we should focus on something else like autonomy and the ability to prosecute, and what it looks like practically, or on the ground. And the Court has consistently rejected a test along those lines, and I think that we have three data points for that.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. They haven't issued that. In all of those cases, Grafton and all of the ones pre짯1952, the Court pointed to the fact that, when these islands pass laws, they could be vetoed by Congress or 짯짯 or were 짯짯 that was the Organic Act. In all of these places, Congress was appointing their legislature, in part, or they were appointing colonial governors. And we went through, in each one of them, very carefully, what the issues of control that remained, that didn't make them sovereign in any way, that made them classic territories. So it's not that we didn't 짯짯 we eschewed looking at that.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: I don't think that's right. And I'd like to just look at the specific example of Puerto Rico that the Court considered in the Shell case. As you say, by that point, Puerto Rico 짯짯 or as you suggested, there had already been significant self짯government in Puerto Rico. There were two houses of the legislature elected. And the Court, when asking this question about whether there could be both Puerto Rico antitrust defenses and Federal antitrust defenses, went through the situation in Puerto Rico and said there is significant self짯government in here now. It defined Puerto Rico as having an autonomy similar to that of the States 짯짯 that's in 1937 짯짯 and said that Congress had given it a sweeping grant of legislative authority. But the Court nonetheless said that under our constitutional system, as a territory of the United States subject to Congress's authority, that Puerto Rico was not a separate sovereign.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But you forget that Congress could veto those laws.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: It 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Before 1952, Congress could veto Puerto Rico's laws. It has relinquished that right.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: I don't think that that's right. I don't think that that's right, and to the extent the Petitioner suggests it, it's just not consistent with the Territory Clause of the Constitution, which, after all 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, now, this is 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: 짯짯 gives plenary power to Congress.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: This is 짯짯 it's very interesting what you're saying. Remember, though, one of the provisions of the Puerto Rico Constitution, which Congress approved and said it was a Republican form of government, is that criminal actions shall be conducted in the name and by the authority of the people of Puerto Rico. Now, that sounds like a delegation of authority as to source, to go back to the Spanish system if they want. Now, if I take your view, then I guess you have to say 짯짯 and it has considerable implication 짯짯 that that doesn't matter because Congress can take back what they gave. Now, is that the position of the government or the executive branch? Because that has tremendous implication.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Because obviously, there is an argument as to whether what Congress and the President gave in Resolution 600, followed by the Constitution, followed by what happened at the UN, under the authority 짯짯 and looking to Felix Frankfurter for guidance, who said that the Constitution provides us with many forms of possible relationship. That's what his view was. And now, is the position of the executive branch 짯짯 I mean, you want to take a position on this? That 짯짯 that 짯짯 that Congress, if it wishes, can take all of that back and Puerto Rico has no more independence than 짯짯 in principle, than any of the other places that were territories? Now, that's 짯짯 that's a big question. But do 짯짯 do you 짯짯 do you see it's an important question? And I want to know if the government's position rests upon it, because that's 짯짯 that's an important statement for the executive, in my opinion.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: Well, two 짯짯 two responses to that question. The first, I think, is the first part of your question, this statement in the Puerto Rico Constitution that the authority to prosecute comes from the people of Puerto Rico and that it's in the name of the people of Puerto Rico. That's been true since 1900. That was in the 1900 Organic Act; that was true in 1917. Puerto Rico is not claiming that it was a sovereign then. So I 짯짯 I would not rely on that. But the second and, obviously, more weighty question you raised is the question of could 짯짯 could Congress revise the arrangements it has with Puerto Rico? And we think the answer is yes, and that that follows from the structure of the Constitution and its history. I want to explain why that's why, but first, I think it's very important for us to say that Congress's position towards Puerto Rico, starting in 1900 through 1917 with the elected legislature 짯짯 elected legislators; 1947, the elected governor; and then this act in 1950, has been one of increasing self짯government, recognizing the benefits of that to the people of Puerto Rico. That's why Congress authorized the enactment of the Constitution. We think that that's a good thing. We have no reason to believe that Congress would revisit that. And we think it's had many benefits for the people of Puerto Rico and the United States. But asking the constitutional question, which is what the Court has asked in its double jeopardy cases, about whether Congress could revise the arrangement with Puerto Rico, the answer is yes. And we think that that follows from its status as a United States territory because of two parts of the Territory Clause. The first is that territories belong to the United States, meaning that they are under the sovereignty of the United States. And then second, that Congress is the one who makes the rules. And if I could just make one more point, which is historically, the fact that Congress was 짯짯 that the United States was the sovereign and the only sovereign in the territories was very important at the time that the Constitution was put together. You may recall that the United States had land there through the Northwest Ordinance, but it was trying to figure out what to do with, and there were questions with the States, what would happen. And so that this provision needed to be added to the Constitution to make clear that Congress was the one who was going to make rules for the territories, and that it would be under the sovereignty of the United States.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But there are some things 짯짯

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: 짯짯 and in this 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Let's go back to the insular cases right there. There are different kinds of territories. It's because of that that Frankfurter says that the Constitution has left the field of invention open. The decisions in the insular cases mean this, if they mean anything: That there's nothing in the Constitution to hamper the responsibility of Congress in working out arrangements. Now, if that's so, why couldn't Congress delegate, without the power to take it back, the authority to Puerto Rico to work out its own criminal code subject to the constraints of the Bill of Rights, et cetera? Why couldn't it? I mean, if Frankfurter is right. Or do you take the opposite position, that it couldn't?

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: The insular cases were talking about something different and narrower, which is whether all the protections of the Bill of Rights apply to the territories by their own force. But to answer the broader question that you answered, or asked, we think that it's inconsistent with the grant of authority to Congress in the Constitution for a territory to be a territory but no longer be sovereign, because the Territory Clause defines territories of the United States as subject to the authority of the United States.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Do you think it's not possible, Ms. Saharsky 짯짯 I mean, putting aside whether Congress has done it here, but you think it's simply not possible for Congress to confer sovereignty in the sense that would matter for the Double Jeopardy Clause?

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: Well, it certainly could by making it a State 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: 짯짯 or by making it independent.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But 짯짯 but 짯짯 but not short of that.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: We think that that would just be fundamentally inconsistent with the constitutional design. And if I could just maybe give the 짯짯 the last part of my historical answer, because I think it's very important, is: When this provision was put into the Constitution, it was coupled with the New States Clause, and it was understood that the options for sovereignty were statehood. It was not that if this was a territory, that the territory was controlled by the United States, and I think it would have been very surprising to the States in the constitutional conventions to think that when States were defined as such important things in the Constitution, sharing the sovereignty with the United States, that Congress could somehow create a sovereign territory. We just don't think that's correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Suppose Congress could also say, could it not, that 짯짯 in its criminal code, that if a crime has been prosecuted and under Puerto Rican law it will not be 짯짯 the same 짯짯 the same crime will not be prosecuted under Federal law?

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: Well, I think there 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Couldn't it say that if it wanted to?

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: I think there are two different options. Congress can define what crimes are and whether they are crimes in Puerto Rico, as opposed to somewhere else. So if that's what you're suggesting, yes, I think that's what Congress could do. What we don't think that Congress could do is change the meaning of the Fifth Amendment and the Double Jeopardy Clause, because that's something for this Court.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: No 짯짯 well, but 짯짯 no, certainly Congress cannot 짯짯 cannot deny double jeopardy effect to something that would be double jeopardy, but I'm talking about a statute that says, even though it might have double jeopardy effect, we say 짯짯 we say no.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: Well 짯짯 right, and what I'm saying is I think Congress' ability is to define crimes and to set out what 짯짯 what they are and where they are, but if the question is what the Fifth Amendment permits and doesn't permit, we really think that's up to this Court.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, it's not a matter of what the Fifth Amendment permits. Congress just says, we will 짯짯 the Justice Department will not prosecute a crime that has already been prosecuted in 짯짯 in Puerto Rico.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: Right. And the Justice Department can work with Puerto Rico to decide who will prosecute what crimes. In fact, we do that as a general matter so that there's usually not any overlap of the kind that occurred in this case. But I think that's just a very different thing from suggesting that Puerto Rico is a sovereign under the Double Jeopardy Clause.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: No, no, I understand that.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: But they're looking at it 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm just saying, if you like that result, it can be done by the statute.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: Right 짯짯 I'm sorry if I misunderstood the question. I'm just trying to be extra careful, because 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm trying to be helpful.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: I know. I know. I 짯짯 I do understand that now. I just want to make sure that 짯짯

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: I just want to make sure that I'm being clear, because I think this case does raise a lot of important questions, and I do think that this Court's jurisprudence over the past 100 years has been very careful about what it means to be a double jeopardy sovereign 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Could you explain before you sit down when and why the United States changed its position on this question? Because as I understand it, in 짯짯 in more than one brief, took the position that Puerto Rico, for double jeopardy cases, is 짯짯 for the Double Jeopardy Clause is 짯짯 is treated like a State.

turns.name: SAHARSKY
turns.text: That's right. The government took that position in two courts of appeals and defended it in a brief in opposition to this Court, where we also said the issue didn't matter. But since that time, we have revisited this issue, given substantial attention to it, both within the Department of Justice and within many agencies of the Federal government, and our position as set out in our brief is that it is not a separate sovereign. And we think that that's entirely consistent with the other things we've learned and the testimony that DOJ and others have given to Congress about Puerto Rico's current status and options for the future. The end of our brief talks about the task force that the President has put in place, that has issued reports three times over the past decade and a half, which have said this is the constitutional status Puerto Rico has now. To the extent the people of Puerto Rico want to change it, here are the options that are consistent with the Constitution. Tell us what you want, and then Congress will decide where to go from there. So I would not want to suggest that as a result of this case, that Puerto Rico's options are set in stone. We don't think that they are set in stone. We just think that right now Puerto Rico is a territory of the United States, and as a result it's not a separate sovereign under the Double Jeopardy Clause. And just to give the court the three data points where the Court has said we're going to focus on sovereignty under the Constitution and we're not going to focus on autonomy or level of control, you have the Puerto Rico v. Shell case which was where the Court said Puerto Rico already has autonomy, but they're still not a double jeopardy sovereign. You have Waller, which is about municipalities and the State of Florida trying to basically treat its municipalities as a sovereign, and the Court said, no, no, no, you are not in the same relationship to your municipalities as the States are to the Federal government; the municipalities are more like the territories, there's a different ultimate source of authority. And then the Court, with respect to the Indian tribes, said even though Congress has the ability to legislate for the tribes, they still have this inherent source of authority that predated the Constitution and was recognized in the Constitution. So for the Court to turn away from that, we think, would really upend that precedent, and not for a good reason, because 짯짯 I think I'd like to conclude, if I can, where I started, which is: This power, this dual sovereignty power, is a weighty power the Court has reserved for those entities that have the ultimate power under our Constitution, defined in our Constitution. And for that reason, Puerto Rico, despite its significant self짯government, is not a sovereign under the Constitution.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Four minutes, Mr. Landau.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. If I could very briefly just say that there's two real questions here, as the questioning has brought out. First, what happened in 1950 to '52? And B, if so, is that constitutional? There's no question, if you look at the documents of 1950 to '52, that Congress required Puerto Rico to have a Republican form of government, which is a government by the people, of the people. This was understood at the time to be a new experiment. This was not just another organic act. And the Constitution of Puerto Rico, which was approved by Congress and the President, says the power, the political power of the Commonwealth emanates from the people. And Congress recognized that. Okay. So that goes to the what happened. Then you go to the real meat of the case, which is the constitutional argument. It is shocking that the Respondents and the United States government in this case are using the Territorial Clause as a restriction on power, a limitation on power of government. This is so ironic, for exactly what Justice Breyer was saying: That the insular cases, if they stand for anything, mean that Congress has plenary control over the territories. That means that Congress can come up with inventive solutions which are broader than the only menu that they give, which is a colony governed pursuant to direct or delegated Federal power, statehood, or independence. Where do they get this view that you can't come up with something inventive like the Commonwealth, or the estado libre asociado, as Justice Breyer said? This is the genius of our system, that it allows us to have these unincorporated territories 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Landau, would you explain what "estado libre asociado" means in Spanish?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: It means "free associated state," literally, and again, I think 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Literally.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: 짯짯 it has the concept of free and it has the concept of associated, and state, in estado.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Do you know why they didn't use that phrase in the compact with the United States?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: I think they thought that 짯짯 that Commonwealth was the more natural English word. I'm not sure, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I believe, because I've seen the Act, that they didn't because States have a different meaning in the United States.

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: And again, we are certainly not saying that we are a State. Again, that was the whole genius of the Commonwealth. It allows flexibility by Congress to come up with these kind of creative solutions. So 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The Solicitor General's office claims that Grafton says that when we're looking at double jeopardy, we're looking at something different. We're looking at territory versus state. Do you agree with that reading of the Shell case and of Grafton?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Absolutely do not agree that Grafton said that in any and all contexts, that regardless of any kind of territory, it is invariably going to have that political relationship. Grafton was describing the particular relationship there, where the Governor of Puerto 짯짯 of the Philippines was appointed by the President. He 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And how about Shell?

turns.name: LANDAU
turns.text: Just to finish that one point, Your Honor, Grafton 짯짯 it was called Grafton v. United States because the Phillipine prosecution was brought in the name of the United States. Shell is exactly the same thing. The Shell case said that was pursuant to an Organic Act, but things changed in the 50s fundamentally. I'm not just saying this. This court recognized in the whole series of decisions in the '70s that there was a fundamental transformation and that Puerto Rico represents a very unique constitutional experiment in our history, about how we have an unincorporated territory that exists in long짯term association with the United States, but being the creator of its own government, which is very important to the people of 짯짯 of the 짯짯 of the government. If there's one thing you read, please, in our reply brief, read that Frankfurter memo because he, as the law officer in the Department of War, addressed exactly this issue. And please do not take the Constitution of Puerto Rico away from the people of Puerto Rico.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
